method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The modern Icelandic name of the country is Ísland, pronounced approximately “Ee-sland,” and is commonly explained as combining the elements for “ice” and “land.”[2][4]  
In the medieval Icelandic sources *Íslendingabók* and *Landnámabók*, the country’s naming is associated with Norse explorers, and later tradition credits Hrafna-Flóki Vilgerðarson with giving Iceland its enduring name after other earlier names such as *Snæland* and *Garðarshólmur* fell out of use.[2][4]  
A popular explanation of the name links it to Flóki’s report of ice encountered in the country, although some accounts note that the name’s form in other languages has been interpreted as related to “island” rather than ice.[2][4]  
By the time the name Ísland became established, it had already replaced earlier proposed names and was in regular use in the Norse settlement period.[4]

==History==
Human settlement in Iceland began in the late 9th century, and the first permanent settler is generally identified as Ingólfur Arnarson, who arrived in 874 CE.[4] Earlier temporary visits by Irish-Scottish monks have also been suggested, although they are thought to have ended when Norse settlers began arriving.[4] The island’s early society was shaped by Viking-age expansion and by a mixed Norse and Celtic population, which also fostered later voyages to Greenland and Vinland on the North American coast.[3] 

Iceland’s recorded history is closely intertwined with its geologically active environment, and written accounts of eruptions preserve a detailed record extending back to the settlement era.[2] Combined with lava fields, vent structures, and tephra layers, these sources have allowed researchers to reconstruct historical volcanic activity with unusual precision.[2] The historical record includes 205 documented volcanic events, among them major long-lived eruptions that lasted months or years and produced lava volumes ranging from 0.01 to 20 km³.[2] 

Among the best-known eruptions are the Eldgjá eruption of 934–940 and the Laki eruption of 1783–1784, which were the two largest volcanic events in Iceland’s history and among the largest of their kind on Earth in the last two millennia.[2] Iceland’s broader geological history is also tied to its position above an active mantle plume, which has shaped the island’s volcanic belts and the wider North Atlantic Igneous Province.[2] 

Earthquakes have also been a recurring feature of Icelandic life, though they have rarely caused severe damage because modern buildings are generally designed to withstand strong shocks.[3] The country’s physical environment, with abundant geothermal activity and frequent seismic events, has therefore played a central role in both its historical development and its modern infrastructure.[3][5]

==Geography==
Iceland is a North Atlantic island country situated between Greenland and the United Kingdom, just south of the Arctic Circle.[2][5] Its position astride the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and over a hotspot gives rise to frequent volcanic and geothermal activity, as well as earthquakes and geysers.[1][3][5] The island’s terrain is dominated by plateaus, mountains, lava fields, ice fields, and fertile lowlands, with a coastline marked by fjords, rugged cliffs, and black-sand beaches.[1][2][4]  

Glaciers cover roughly one-tenth of the country, with the largest ice caps concentrated in the central highlands.[1][3][4][5] Large areas of the interior are volcanic desert, and much of the central highlands remains sparsely populated or uninhabited.[1][3] The island’s glacial and volcanic processes continue to reshape its landscapes, including waterfalls, canyons, geothermal areas, and active volcanic systems.[1][4][5]  

Although Iceland is relatively small, its land use is limited by terrain and climate: pasture and grazing areas are concentrated near the coasts, and cultivation occupies only a small fraction of the land.[1]

==Politics==
Iceland is a constitutional republic and parliamentary democracy with a multi-party system.[3][4] Executive power is exercised by the government, while the president serves as head of state in a largely ceremonial role.[3][4] The Althingi, Iceland’s unicameral parliament, has 63 members elected by proportional representation for four-year terms, and the president invites the leader best placed to form a cabinet after elections.[3][4]

The president is elected by direct popular vote for a four-year term, with no term limit.[3] Although the office is mainly symbolic, presidents may use their role to encourage public discussion on national issues.[2][3] As of August 2024, Halla Tómasdóttir is president and Bjarni Benediktsson is prime minister.[2]

The constitutional framework in force since 1944 places real political power in parliament and the cabinet, which must retain majority support or avoid censure.[4] Citizens enjoy civil rights characteristic of Western democracies, and the judiciary is independent of the executive and legislature.[4] Iceland’s courts comprise a supreme court and lower courts, with judges appointed and no jury system.[4]

Local government is responsible for primary education, municipal services, and social programs, and municipalities are governed by elected councils.[4] The country’s political system has also been shaped by its older parliamentary traditions, with the Althingi originally established in 930 and later restored in the 19th century after a long interruption under foreign rule.[3][2]

Contemporary politics has been strongly affected by the financial crisis of 2008, which weakened political trust even after the economy recovered.[1] Since then, party competition has been reshaped, with new parties gaining prominence and coalition patterns shifting, although rural-urban and left-right divisions remain important.[1] Iceland is rated highly in democratic performance overall, but has seen recent declines in economic equality and freedom of expression, particularly in relation to academic freedom.[1]

==Economy==
Iceland has a small but advanced mixed economy shaped by its limited domestic market and heavy reliance on external trade.[2] Tourism, fishing, and aluminum smelting are the main pillars of the economy, with tourism providing the largest share of export earnings in recent years.[2] Marine products and aluminum-related manufacturing also remain important export sectors, while imports are dominated by industrial supplies, capital goods, transportation equipment, fuels, and food products.[2]

The economy was hit hard by the COVID-19 pandemic, which caused a steep contraction in 2020 after several years of strong growth driven by tourism.[2] Recovery was rapid, with GDP rebounding sharply in 2022 and continuing to grow in 2023, though output contracted slightly in 2024.[2] Inflation rose during the pandemic period but eased by 2025, and unemployment remained relatively low by international standards.[2]

Tourism has transformed Iceland’s economy over the past decade and attracted investment in high-end accommodation and related services.[1][2] Visitor numbers fell sharply during the pandemic but later recovered, returning to more than 2 million annually in the mid-2020s.[2] The sector has become central not only to exports but also to domestic employment and business activity.[1][2]

Beyond tourism, Iceland has diversified into manufacturing, software production, biotechnology, and other service industries.[1] Abundant geothermal and hydropower resources have supported foreign investment, especially in aluminum production and in emerging high-tech activities such as data centers.[1] This energy advantage has helped Iceland develop a service-oriented economy with a relatively strong knowledge-intensive sector and a notable role for small and medium-sized enterprises.[4]

Fishing remains economically significant despite the growth of other sectors, and the economy continues to be sensitive to fish stocks and world prices for key exports.[1] Public statistics also show continued importance for fisheries, agriculture, industry, science and technology, and tourism in the structure of the economy.[5]

==Demographics==
Iceland had a population of 387,558 in 2023, and estimates for early 2024 placed it just under 400,000.[4][3] By 2026, the population was estimated at 402,329, with a yearly increase of 1.02%.[1] 

The population has grown rapidly in recent decades, reaching 200,000 in 1968 and 300,000 in 2007, and by early 2024 it was the largest ever recorded in the country.[5] This growth has been driven not only by natural increase but also by immigration, especially since 2005.[5][3] 

In early 2024, slightly more than half of the population was male, while females made up about 48%, and people identifying as non-binary or other genders accounted for 0.04%.[3] 

Iceland’s population is comparatively young by European standards, with a median age of 36.6 years in 2026.[1] The country’s demographic profile is commonly described as having a relatively low fertility rate, low mortality, and high life expectancy, placing it in the later stages of demographic transition.[5] 

Immigration has become an important component of population change, and by February 2024 about 20% of the population had immigrant or foreign backgrounds, with Poles forming the largest immigrant group at 6%.[3] Iceland’s long-term demographic pattern has also shifted from a historically homogeneous population to a more diverse one, with foreign-background residents now making up a substantial share of the total.[3]

==Culture==
Icelandic culture is shaped by a strong emphasis on preserving historical sites, cultural artefacts, and national heritage for future generations.[1] The state also supports museums and national cultural institutions, which it uses to promote public access to the country’s past and to encourage independent research in history and culture.[1]

The Ministry of Culture and Business Affairs is responsible for the arts and wider cultural policy, and the government says its role is to create conditions in which artistic variety, creativity, and initiative can flourish.[1] State support for the arts is intended to make cultural life accessible to all residents regardless of social status and to provide a favourable working environment for artists.[1]

Government involvement in the arts is focused mainly on operating state-run cultural institutions and supporting professional artists.[1] The same ministry also oversees youth affairs outside formal education, supports youth organisations, and conducts research in this area.[1] In addition, sports fall under its remit, including data collection on sporting activity and facilities as well as oversight of anti-doping programmes.[1]

The media are also administered under the Ministry’s auspices, including the Media Commission and the Icelandic National Broadcasting Service (RÚV).[1] This places broadcasting and media regulation within the same broad cultural framework as the arts and heritage sector.[1]

Icelandic cultural identity has also been strongly influenced by historical and literary traditions, particularly the revival of older national symbols and celebrations in the modern era.[2] At the same time, contemporary Icelandic culture is described as increasingly international, especially as most of the population now lives in the capital area.[2]

Education and research play an important role in cultural life, with the University of Iceland serving as a central institution for scientific research and higher learning.[2] Its development into a university with faculties including philosophy, engineering, and social sciences reflects the broad institutional base of modern Icelandic intellectual culture.[2]

Folklore remains a visible part of the country’s cultural landscape, including beliefs and stories about elves, trolls, sea monsters, and land spirits.[3][5] Such traditions continue to be presented as an important expression of Icelandic mythology and identity.[3]

Popular descriptions of Icelandic culture also highlight recurring customs and festive traditions, such as Bóndadagur, Konudagur, and Þorrablót, which feature communal meals, songs, games, and storytelling.[4] These practices are often presented alongside wider cultural attractions such as music, arts, galleries, and museums.[4]
